children.name: Rana
children.description: The archetypal frogs of the Northern Hemisphere — agile, long-legged leapers of ponds, ditches, and damp grassland. The European Common Frog is one of the first signs of spring across northern Europe, gathering in ponds in late February and March to breed in vast, croaking congregations. Its spawn — masses of black-centred, gel-coated eggs — is one of the most recognisable sights of early spring across Britain and Scandinavia. Rana frogs are sensitive indicators of environmental quality: the Moor Frog's deep-blue breeding males are unmistakable, and the Agile Frog is a reliable indicator of ancient, undisturbed habitat.

children.name: Rana temporaria
children.description: The most familiar amphibian in Britain and much of northern Europe. Breeds earlier than almost any other frog — pond ice may still be present when the first adults arrive to spawn. The adult's colour is highly variable: brown, grey, olive, or even red.

children.name: Rana arvalis
children.description: During a brief breeding window of a few days, males turn an intense cobalt blue — one of the most striking colour transformations in European herpetology. A boreal and steppe species, common in Scandinavia and Russia.

children.name: Rana dalmatina
children.description: One of Europe's most athletic frogs, capable of leaping over 2 metres. Associated with ancient deciduous woodland. A very early breeder — spawns before the Common Frog in some regions. Declining across its range.

children.name: Rana sylvatica
children.description: The only frog found north of the Arctic Circle in North America. Survives freezing solid in winter — up to 65% of body water converts to ice around (not inside) cells. Heart stops completely; body temperature may reach -8°C. Thaws and walks away in spring.

children.name: Pelophylax
children.description: Water frogs — the large, aquatic, green frogs of Europe and western Asia. They spend far more time in water than the brown frogs of the Rana group, and their loud, persistent croaking from the margins of ponds, lakes, and slow rivers is one of the defining sounds of a European summer. The Edible Frog is a stable hybrid between Pool Frog and Marsh Frog that reproduces by a process called hybridogenesis — replacing its own genome with the genome of one parent species each generation in an unusual form of genetic parasitism.

children.name: Pelophylax ridibundus
children.description: Europe's largest native frog. Introduced to Britain in the 1930s from Hungary; now well established in Romney Marsh and other wetlands. Highly vocal — croaking begins in April and continues through summer. Feeds on invertebrates, small fish, and occasionally other frogs.

children.name: Pelophylax lessonae
children.description: Native to Britain but extinct by the 1990s due to habitat loss; reintroduced from Scandinavian stock starting in 2005. The Pool Frog is now a Biodiversity Action Plan species, representing one of the few cases of a recently extinct British vertebrate being reintroduced.

children.name: Pelophylax esculentus
children.description: A stable hybrid of P. lessonae × P. ridibundus that reproduces by hybridogenesis — discarding one parental genome each generation. The origin of the 'cuisses de grenouilles' of French cuisine. Classified as a protected species across much of Europe.

children.name: Lithobates
children.description: American true frogs — the diverse radiation of Ranidae in the New World. The American Bullfrog is the largest frog in North America and one of the world's most damaging invasive species, introduced worldwide for frog-leg production; it consumes virtually anything that fits in its mouth, carries chytrid fungus (*Batrachochytrium dendrobatidis*) without succumbing to it, and has contributed to the extinction or severe decline of native amphibians on every continent where it has been introduced.

children.name: Lithobates catesbeianus
children.description: Listed among the world's 100 worst invasive species. The deep, resonant 'jug-o-rum' call of males carries hundreds of metres. Males defend territories in ponds and may eat ducklings, small snakes, other frogs, and even bats. A carrier of the amphibian-killing chytrid fungus.

children.name: Lithobates pipiens
children.description: One of the most widespread frogs in North America and formerly the most commonly used animal in biology education. Populations declined sharply in the 1970s–80s due to disease, UV exposure through ozone depletion, and habitat loss.
